LED is a novel diffusion-based trajectory prediction model that provides real-time, accurate, and diverse predictions.
LED leverages a trainable leapfrog initializer to directly learn an expressive multi-modal distribution of future trajectories.
The leapfrog initializer is trained to appropriately allocate correlated samples to provide a diversity of predicted future trajectories, improving prediction performances.
Extensive experiments on real-world datasets, including NBA/NFL/SDD/ETH-UCY, show that LED consistently improves performance.
LED speeds up the inference compared to the standard diffusion model on NBA/NFL/SDD/ETH-UCY datasets.

Trajectory prediction aims to predict an agent's future trajectory based on past trajectories of itself and surrounding agents.
X represents the observed past trajectory over multiple timestamps.
Y represents the corresponding ground-truth future trajectory for the to-be-predicted agent over multiple timestamps.
yt is the 2D coordinate at future timestamp t in R2.
Because of the indeterminacy of future trajectories, it is usually more reliable to predict more than one trajectory to capture multiple possibilities.
Stochastic trajectory prediction predicts the distribution of a future trajectory, instead of a single future trajectory.
The goal of stochastic trajectory prediction is to train a prediction model gθ(·) with parameters θ to generate a distribution.
Based on distribution Pθ, we can draw K samples, Y = {Y1,Y2,...,YK}.
The overall learning problem is D(Yi, Y), s.t. Y ∼ Pθ.
Diffusion model is presented as a standard model for trajectory prediction.
Diffusion model lays a foundation for the proposed method of trajectory prediction.
Diffusion model performs a series of simple denoising steps to refine trajectories.
Diffusion model performs a forward diffusion process to intentionally add noise to a ground-truth future trajectory.
Diffusion model uses a conditional denoising process to recover the future trajectory from noise inputs conditioned on past trajectories.
Let X and XN be the past trajectories of the ego agent and the neighboring agents, respectively.

Trajectory is the path followed by an agent, where past trajectories of ego agent and neighboring agents are considered along with the future trajectory of the ego agent in the context of diffusion model for trajectory prediction.
The diffusion model is used for trajectory prediction by initializing diffused trajectories and using forward diffusion operation to add noises to past trajectories to obtain the diffused trajectory.
Supervision is provided for the trajectory at each diffusion step to guide the denoising process and obtain the final denoised trajectory.
Denoising step in the process is the reverse of the diffusion step, where each denoising step aims to obtain the denoised trajectory and shares the same underlying distribution.
The denoising step is the reverse of the diffusion step conceptually.
Each k shares the same underlying distribution.
The standard diffusion model is powerful in learning sophisticated distributions.

Motion prediction requires real-time inference.
The running time of a diffusion model is constrained by the large number of denoising steps.
Less denoising steps usually cause a weaker representation ability of future distributions.
To achieve higher efficiency while preserving a promising representation ability, the leapfrog diffusion model is proposed.
Leapfrog diffusion model uses a trainable initializer to capture sophisticated distributions.
The leapfrog diffusion model substitutes a large number of denoising steps to achieve higher efficiency.
The leapfrog diffusion model significantly accelerates the inference speed without losing representation ability.
